does: This paper introduces perturbation probing, a lightweight causal discovery method that identifies task-specific functional roles of feed-forward neurons in large language models using only two forward passes per prompt and no backpropagation. The method discovers neurons whose intervention produces behavioral changes, then validates causal importance through ablation sweeps. Across eight behavioral tasks and 13 models, the authors identify two core circuit structures: opposition circuits where RLHF suppresses pre-training behaviors, exemplified by safety refusal controlled by approximately 50 neurons (0.014% of total) that determine response formatting on 520 AdvBench prompts with minimal harmful compliance, and routing circuits for pre-training behaviors distributed through attention, demonstrated by language selection where residual-stream injection switches output from English to Chinese in models meeting specific architectural conditions.
connects: This work builds on mechanistic interpretability research examining how neural circuits implement specific behaviors in LLMs, extending prior work on neuron importance, circuit analysis, and causal interventions in transformer models. It responds to limitations of backpropagation-based attribution methods by proposing a gradient-free alternative suitable for scaling analysis across many models and architectures. The distinction between opposition circuits arising from RLHF training dynamics and routing circuits from pre-training connects to broader understanding of how alignment procedures modify model internals versus preserving base model structure. The focus on safety refusal circuits relates to recent mechanistic studies of how models implement alignment behaviors, while the two-pass efficiency builds on efforts to make interpretability techniques computationally tractable for large-scale model analysis.
enables: This method establishes a scalable framework for discovering causal circuit structures across diverse behavioral domains and model variants, facilitating systematic comparison of how different architectures and training procedures produce behavioral outputs. The identification of minimal neuron subsets controlling high-level behaviors like safety refusal opens pathways for targeted model editing and intervention strategies without full retraining. The taxonomy of opposition versus routing circuits provides conceptual vocabulary for understanding alignment mechanisms and could enable prediction of which circuits arise under different training regimes. The demonstration that specific architectural properties predict successful routing circuit injection suggests strategies for controlled behavioral modification, while the efficiency of the method enables future work mapping behavioral circuits across scaling dimensions, investigating how circuit complexity changes with model capacity, and developing interpretability-informed approaches to model safety and alignment.
